    ← VibesMERIDIAN & CO.
    0

    The Storefront

    Goods that actually add to cart.

    Everything on this page actually works -- add items, switch colours, adjust quantities, watch the total move. It's the one demo with real state behind it.

    ❯ Filter & sortSituational

    Essential once a catalogue gets big -- it's basically how people cope with hundreds of items. On a curated shop of six products though, it's just friction pretending to be help.

    How it's done

    Filter toggles the visible cards by category; sort reorders them by price or rating in place.

    4.5 (156)
    ❯ Product gridEssential

    The retail workhorse, basically. A scannable grid of image + name + price + rating is what shoppers expect, and how they actually compare things. Not much point reinventing this one.

    How it's done

    A responsive CSS grid of reusable ProductCard components -- each carrying its own price, variants, rating, and stock.

    NewOnly 3 left

    Canvas Tote

    $38

    4.8 (64)

    Hover-swap

    Hover the card -- the image flips to a second angle. Seeing the back, or a detail shot, answers a real question before the shopper even has to ask it.

    ❯ Image hover-swapEssential

    One of the few 'flourishes' that genuinely helps a purchase decision. A second view actually reduces returns and hesitation. Worth the effort here.

    How it's done

    Two stacked images; the back one fades in on :hover with a CSS opacity transition.

    Oak Lounge Chair

    $340

    4.7 (88)

    Variant swatches

    Click a colour dot -- the product recolours instantly, and that choice follows the item right into the cart. Let people pick what they actually want.

    ❯ Variant swatchesEssential

    If a product comes in variants, showing them up front is basically non-negotiable. Hiding colours behind a click just loses sales. The selected variant has to carry through to the cart.

    How it's done

    Each swatch sets the card's --pc custom property (recolouring the gradient) and stores the choice in data-color for add-to-cart.

    Sale

    $120 $89

    Sale badge & strikethrough

    A crossed-out "was" price makes the brain read "deal" instantly. It works -- and that's exactly why it's regulated.

    ❯ Sale badge & strikethroughSituational

    Powerful and legitimate when the 'was' price was actually real. Invented or permanent 'sales' are a legal and trust risk in most markets -- don't fake the anchor.

    How it's done

    A strikethrough original price beside the current one, plus a badge. The comparison does the persuading.

    4.6 · 128 reviews

    Star ratings

    Quantified social proof, right where the decision happens. Half-filled stars and a review count carry more weight than any adjective you could write.

    ❯ Star ratingsSituational

    Only as trustworthy as the reviews actually behind them. Real ratings convert -- decorative five-star rows with no reviews just read as fake, and can backfire.

    How it's done

    Two layered star strings -- a grey base and a gold overlay clipped to (rating ÷ 5) width.

    tap to save

    Wishlist

    Tap the heart on any product to save it -- the count in the header keeps a running tally that sticks around as you browse. Try it on the grid above.

    ❯ WishlistSituational

    A save-for-later heart is genuinely useful on a store people actually come back to -- basically a memory that spans visits. On a site nobody returns to though, it's just saving things into a void. It's worth exactly as much as your return traffic; without that, it implies a relationship the shop doesn't really have.

    How it's done

    Clicking a heart writes the product id to sessionStorage and updates the header count. Real builds tie it to an account so the list survives across devices; ours survives the session -- enough to feel the pattern.

    Only 2 left

    Scarcity cue

    "Only 2 left" adds pressure to decide right now. It's effective -- and honestly the feature most often faked.

    ❯ Scarcity cue⚠ Manipulative

    Real low stock is useful information. Fabricated or perpetual 'only 2 left' counters are a dark pattern though -- they convert short-term, and erode trust the second someone notices.

    How it's done

    A stock badge shown only when real inventory drops below a threshold. Ours reflects the demo product's actual stock number.

    The cart is real. Add a few things, then reopen it.

    Cart drawer

    Add-to-cart slides open a drawer with live quantities, a running subtotal, a free-shipping meter -- no full-page reload, no losing your place.

    ❯ Cart drawerEssential

    A slide-over cart keeps people shopping instead of bouncing off to a checkout page. Instant feedback -- the count bump, the moving total -- is honestly what makes a store feel trustworthy and alive.

    How it's done

    Real client-side state: a variant-keyed line-item list that re-renders the drawer, subtotal, count badge, and shipping meter on every change.

    Recently viewed

    Tap any product's image, or add it to the cart, and it lands here -- a running memory of what caught your eye, kept for the session.

    Nothing yet -- tap a product image above and it'll show up here.

    ❯ Recently viewedSituational

    A low-key memory of what you looked at, so you can double back without hunting for it. Genuinely handy in a big catalogue; pure clutter in a shop of six things you can already see at once. Situational -- and quietly one of the most-clicked rows in real retail, for what it's worth.

    How it's done

    Each product you tap is pushed to the front of a short list in sessionStorage (newest first, de-duplicated, capped at five). Real builds tie it to an account; ours lasts the session.

    That's the Storefront.

    The one that actually works.

    ← Back to the vibesGet this for my brand →
    Next vibeThe Statement →
    Your cart (0)

    ❯ Free-shipping meterSituational

    Probably the most defensible nudge in commerce: 'you're $12 away' grows the order, but the reward's real and the math is honest. It only turns manipulative once the threshold starts moving. (Yes, the narration follows you into the cart.)

    How it's done

    Re-rendered with the cart on every change -- the bar's fill is subtotal ÷ threshold, and the message counts down the real remaining gap.

      Your cart is empty -- add something from the grid.

      Subtotal$0
      • Free shipping over $75
      • Secure checkout
      • 30-day returns
